reasoning: The Polymarket price for 'no rate cuts' was 17% on July 24. Since then, two major developments have aggressively shifted expectations toward imminent rate cuts. First, two FOMC members (Bowman and Waller) dissented at the July 30 meeting, preferring an immediate 25 bps cut, signaling strong internal momentum for easing. Second, the August 1 jobs report was surprisingly weak, with only 73k jobs added, heavy downward revisions to prior months, and an uptick in unemployment to 4.2%. Consequently, market-implied odds (via CME FedWatch) for a September rate cut skyrocketed to over 80%, with a majority of traders pricing in at least two cuts by year-end. Given that the baseline probability was 17% prior to this dovish shock, the true probability of zero cuts through December must now be well below 10%. I estimate a 6% chance that inflation rebounds sufficiently to force the Fed into a holding pattern for the remainder of 2025.
